MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-26-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Plaintiff David Jah, Jr. telephone number 213-884-3382 appeared in Propria Persona.
James Hannawalt of Office of the City Attorney telephone number 415-554-3913 for Defendant City and County of San Francisco.

The Court explained process to Mr. Jah. Mr. Hannawalt argued the matter. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied. The City alternate motion for summary adjudication is: a) granted as to the third cause of action for aiding and abetting, the fifth cause of action for false imprisonment, the eighth cause of action for gross negligence, the ninth cause of action for violation of the Public Records Act and the tenth, eleventh and twelfth causes of action for violations of the California Constitution and b) denied as to the first cause of action for battery, second cause of action for assault, the fourth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress, the sixth cause of action for violation of the Bane Act and the seventh cause of action for negligence. As to first, second, fourth and sixth causes of action, plaintiff David Jah has presented admissible evidence in the form of his declaration that creates triable issues whether the City used excessive force and acted through "threats, intimidation, or coercion." Aiding and abetting is a basis to impose liability, not a separate claim, and in the context of this case the third cause of action is duplicative of the first and second causes of action. The City is entitled to judgment on the fifth cause of action because the undisputed facts show that the police had probable cause to believe that plaintiff committed a public offence in his presence by delaying a police investigation. (Penal Code 148 and 836(a); Chu Dec. par. 4.). The seventh cause is alleged against former Chief Suhr and is a cognizable claim under Government Code 815.2. The court grants summary adjudication as to the negligence and gross negligence causes of action. There is no cognizable "cause of action" for gross negligence. (City of Santa Barbara v. Superior Court (2007) 41 Cal.4th 747, 779-780; Jimenez v. 24 Hour Fitness USA, Inc. (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 546, 552, n.3.) The City is entitled to judgment on the ninth and tenth causes of action because the undisputed facts show that plaintiff has received the police report. (Plaintiff's Responding Separate Statement, Ninth Cause of Action, Fact 1). The City is also entitled to judgment on the tenth, eleventh and twelfth causes of action because there is not private right of action for damages for violations of the portions of the California Constitution that are the predicates for those claims. (Javor v. Taggart (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 795, 807.) Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
